author: yuxu42
title: JS API E2E Electron test cleanup fails with Permission Denied on electron.exe on Windows
signature: rm cannot remove electron.exe Permission denied|Flaky Test

failed_jobs.name: JS API (24)
failed_jobs.symptom: E2E 'after all' cleanup hook fails: rm cannot remove electron.exe: Permission denied

key_errors:
exec error: Error: Command failed: rm -rf demo-electron-app-project
rm: cannot remove 'demo-electron-app-project/node_modules/electron/dist/electron.exe': Permission denied

1) E2E testing for OpenVINO as an Electron dependency.
     "after all" hook for "should run electron package and verify output":
   Error: Command failed: rm -rf demo-electron-app-project
rm: cannot remove 'demo-electron-app-project/node_modules/electron/dist/electron.exe': Permission denied
root_cause: The mocha E2E test suite's 'after all' cleanup hook runs `rm -rf demo-electron-app-project` via a child_process shell command. On Windows, the Electron binary (electron.exe) downloaded into node_modules gets locked by the OS after being executed in the test. Git Bash's `rm` cannot delete Windows locked executables, so the cleanup fails with Permission Denied. The actual E2E tests (install dependencies, run electron package) both passed; only the teardown fails. This is a known Windows file-locking behavior with Electron binaries and does not indicate a code regression.
reproduction_steps: Run `npm run test:e2e` in src/bindings/js/node on a Windows runner. The cleanup hook calls `rm -rf demo-electron-app-project` which may fail due to Windows file locking on electron.exe after it has been executed.
recommended_actions: Use `rimraf` npm package or Node.js `fs.rm` with recursive option instead of `rm -rf` shell command in the 'after all' hook — these tools properly handle Windows file deletion semantics.
Alternatively, use PowerShell `Remove-Item -Recurse -Force` on Windows runners, or add a `taskkill /F /IM electron.exe /T` step before cleanup to ensure the process is terminated.
Add error handling in the 'after all' hook so a cleanup failure does not fail the entire test suite (use try/catch around the cleanup exec call).
Re-run the failed jobs: this is a transient Windows file-locking issue that clears between runs.
prevention_strategies: Replace the `rm -rf` shell command in the E2E test cleanup hook with a cross-platform Node.js solution (e.g., `fs.rmSync(path, {recursive: true, force: true})`) or the `rimraf` package. This prevents cleanup failures caused by Windows file locking of Electron executables. Alternatively, wrap the cleanup in try/catch so it does not fail the overall test run.
historical_context: This is the first recorded occurrence of this specific failure pattern in the CI Doctor MQ database. Windows file-locking issues with Electron binaries are a known general problem in Electron testing pipelines.
